title: Same Lottery Numbers Every Draw: The 6-Year Truth
description: Playing the same Powerball numbers every Wednesday since 2020 cost exactly $676. Here's the dollar-by-dollar breakdown of what came back.
content:
338 Draws. One Ticket. Zero Jackpots.

Here's the number that should stop you cold: 338. That's how many Wednesday Powerball draws took place between January 2020 and July 2026. And if you played the exact same five numbers plus a Powerball every single one of those Wednesdays — never missing, never switching — you spent $676 and never once matched all six numbers.

Not even close. But the story of what did happen is stranger, and more instructive, than the jackpot you never hit.

Why People Play the Same Numbers Every Week

Before we get into the data, let's talk about why anyone would ask the question: what if same lottery numbers every draw? It isn't irrational. It's deeply human.

There's a psychological phenomenon researchers call the \"gambler's sense of ownership\" — the feeling that a set of numbers you've claimed somehow belongs to you. Birthday dates. A childhood address. The jersey number of a player who once made you believe in magic. Switching feels like abandonment. What if they come up the week you stopped playing them?

That fear is real. The math behind it is not. But let's run the simulation anyway, because the actual numbers are far more interesting than the fear.

The Running Tally — What 338 Draws Actually Looked Like

For this simulation, we used a representative ticket: 7, 18, 23, 52, 64 + PB 10 — a mix of current hot numbers (#18, #52, and #64 each appeared 13 times in the last 100 draws) and the chronically cold #23, which as of today has gone 57 consecutive draws without appearing. That's the most overdue number in the entire Powerball pool right now.

Across 338 draws, here's the statistical reality for any fixed five-number combination in Powerball. The odds of matching just the Powerball alone sit at 1 in 38.32. Matching one white ball plus the Powerball: 1 in 91.98. Across 338 tries, a player should statistically expect roughly 3 to 5 minor prize hits — mostly $4 Powerball-only matches or the occasional $7 one-white-ball-plus-Powerball result.

That's not a windfall. That's a slow bleed punctuated by the occasional reminder that the machine noticed you existed.

What-If Simulator Results — 338 Wednesday Draws

Draw RangeDraws PlayedTotal SpentEstimated HitsPrize TypeEstimated Return
Jan 2020 – Dec 202052$1041PB only ($4)$4
Jan 2021 – Dec 202152$10411 white + PB ($7)$7
Jan 2022 – Dec 202252$1040No prize$0
Jan 2023 – Dec 202352$1041PB only ($4)$4
Jan 2024 – Dec 202452$1041PB only ($4)$4
Jan 2025 – Jul 202678$1561PB only ($4)$4
TOTAL338$6765Mixed minor prizes$23

The Number That Makes This Real

After 338 consecutive Wednesday Powerball draws — six and a half years of loyalty to the same ticket — the simulated player recouped just $23 of their $676 investment. That's a return rate of 3.4 cents on every dollar spent. The gap between what went in and what came back: $653.

Read that again. Not $653 lost on a bad night. Lost at a rate of roughly $2 per week, so gradually it barely registered — until you add it all up and see the number staring back at you.

Cumulative Spend vs. Cumulative Winnings — The Chart That Tells the Whole Story

If you plotted these two lines on a graph, you'd see something almost poetic in its bluntness. The spending line climbs in a perfect diagonal — $2 every Wednesday, without exception, from draw 1 to draw 338. The winnings line flatlines for long stretches, then ticks upward by $4 or $7 in a tiny jump, then flatlines again. By draw 338, the spending line sits at $676. The winnings line sits at $23. The white space between them is $653 wide, and it doesn't lie.

This is what playing the same lottery numbers every draw actually looks like when you refuse to let the story stay abstract.

What the Numbers Actually Tell You About Ticket Loyalty

Here's the uncomfortable truth the data delivers: your ticket doesn't remember you. Number 23 — the most overdue ball in the current Powerball pool, absent for 57 straight draws — has no memory of its drought. It isn't building pressure like a steam valve. Each draw is independent. The machine doesn't owe you anything for your patience.

And yet, the hot numbers don't save you either. Including #18, #52, and #64 — each appearing 13 times in the last 100 draws, the hottest trio in the current dataset — doesn't meaningfully change the simulation outcome for a fixed ticket. Hot streaks are descriptions of the past, not prescriptions for the future. You can explore the full frequency breakdown on our Powerball statistics page and see exactly how dramatically frequency shifts from one 100-draw window to the next.

What the 338-draw simulation does tell you is something more honest than any hot-number tip: the cost of loyalty is real, the reward is probabilistic, and the jackpot odds don't compress just because you've been showing up. The odds of hitting the Powerball jackpot remain 1 in 292,201,338 on draw 338 exactly as they were on draw 1.

If you're curious how fixed-ticket simulations play out differently across game formats — shorter odds, smaller pools, more frequent minor prizes — it's worth comparing against Mega Millions statistics side by side. The structure of the prize tiers changes the shape of the return curve even when the jackpot odds are similarly astronomical.

Six and a half years. $676 out the door. $23 back. The numbers don't judge the habit — but they do describe it, precisely and without mercy.
